source_metadata.title_raw: Christine Dietrick - A-19-039 - March 25, 2019 - San Luis Obispo

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Christine Dietrick 
City Attorney 
City of San Luis Obispo 
990 Palm Street 
San Luis Obispo, CA 93401-3249 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-039 
Dear Ms. Dietrick: 
March 25, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of San Luis Obispo City 
Councilmember Erica A. Stewart regarding Government Code Section 1090 and the conflict of 
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing 
advice under those bodies of law and not under any other conflict of interest laws. Also note that we 
are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any 
advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts 
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to it to the Office of the Attorney General and the San Luis Obispo County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We are also required to 
specify whether we have received a written response from either entity: we have not. (Section 
1097 .1 ( c )( 4 ). ) Finally, we are required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the 
following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the 
requestor." (Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTIONS 
(1) Is California Polytechnic State University ("Cal Poly") a "business entity" under the Act? 
(2) Does Councilmember Stewart have a source of income interest in Cal Poly under the Act given 
that she is a salaried, exempt management employee of Cal Poly? 
(3) Does Section 1090 prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the making of, 
or the City from entering into: 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-19-039 
Page No. 2 
(A) The potential extension of an expiring transit agreement between the City and Cal Poly 
governing the City's provision of transit services to Cal Poly and promotion of those 
services to Cal Poly students? 
(B) The potential future modification of an existing emergency services agreement between 
the City and Cal Poly governing the City's provision of fire and emergency medical services 
to Cal Poly? 
CONCLUSIONS 
(1) No. Cal Poly is not a "business entity" under the Act because it does not meet the definition of 
that term set forth in Section 82005. 
(2) No. The Councilmember does not have a source of income interest in Cal Poly under the Act 
because her salary and other similar benefit payments from Cal Poly fall within the "government 
salary exception" to the definition of "income" set forth in Section 82030(b )(2). 
(3) No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in making, 
or the City from entering into, either of those potential contracts because the noninterest exception 
set forth in Section 1091.5(a)(9) applies so long as the Councilmember discloses her interest in Cal 
Poly to the City Council at the time the potential contract is considered and notes the interest in the 
City Council's official record. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
You are the City Attorney for the City of San Luis Obispo and the authorized representative 
of San Luis Obispo City Councilmember Erica A. Stewart. One of the California State University 
system's 23 campuses, Cal Poly is a compreh~nsive public university located immediately adjacent 
to the City's jurisdictional boundaries. The Councilmember is a salaried, exempt management 
employee at Cal Poly, where she works as an Assistant Director for Personnel and Marketing in Cal 
Poly's Campus Health & Wellbeing Department. 
The City will soon consider the potential extension of an expiring transit agreement between 
the City and Cal Poly governing the City's provision of transit services to Cal Poly and promotion 
of those services to Cal Poly students. In the future, the City may also consider modifying an 
existing emergency services agreement between the City and Cal Poly governing the City's 
provision of fire and emergency medical services to Cal Poly if changes to that contract become 
necessary due to significantly changed circumstances. The Councilmember is not anticipated to 
have any role in Cal Poly's negotiation or authorization of either of these potential contracts. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act 
We first address whether Cal Poly is a "business entity" under the Act. Section 82005 
defines "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but not 


File No. A-19-039 
Page No. 3 
limited to a proprietorship, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association." 
Section 82049 defines "state agency" as "every state office, department, division, bureau, board and 
commission, and the Legislature." 
Cal Poly is a comprehensive public university and one of the California State University 
system's 23 campuses. As a public university, Cal Poly is not operated for profit. We have 
previously advised that the California State University is a "state agency" (Knight Advice Letter, 
No. A-99-165), and that an individual campus of the California State University is also a "state 
agency" (Waggoner Advice Letter, No. A-85-146). Therefore, Cal Poly is not a "business entity" 
under the Act because it is not an "organization or enterprise operated for profit." 
We next address whether the Councilmember has a source of income interest in Cal Poly 
because she is a salaried, exempt management employee of the university. The Act's conflict of 
interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a governmental decision if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material financial effect on one or more of 
the official's financial interests. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give 
rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest are identified in Section 87103, and subdivision (c) of that 
section provides that an official has an inte.rest in a source of income aggregating $500 or more to 
the official in the 12 months prior to the decision at issue. . 
Section 82030(b)(2), however, sets forth the "government salary exception" to the Act's 
definition of "income," which provides in pertinent part that "income" does not include salary and 
other similar benefit payments from a state agency. Therefore, the Councilmember does not have a 
source of income interest in Cal Poly because her salary and other similar benefits from Cal Poly 
are not "income" under the Act.2 
Section 1090 
We now turn to consider whether Section 1090 prohibits the Councilmember from making 
or participating in making, or the City from entering into, a transit agreement or an emergency 
services agreement between the City and Cal Poly. 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers and employees, while working in their 
official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is 
concerned with financial interests, other than remote interests or noninterests, that prevent a public 
officer or employee from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best 
interests of his or her agency. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest," and "an official has a financial interest in a contract if he [ or she] might profit 
from it." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is 
void, and the prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and 
2 While we note that an official also has a financial interest in his or her personal finances (Section 87103; 
Regulation 18702.5), there is no indication that the identified decisions will foreseeably affect the Councilmember's 
personal finances. If you need additional assistance determining ifa specific decision may affect the Councilmember's 
personal finances, you should seek additional assistance identifying the decision in question. 


File No. A-19-039 
Page No. 4 
equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 is 
applicable to one member of a governing body of a public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided 
by having the interested board member abstain; the entire governing body is precluded from 
entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.) 
A decision to modify, extend, or renegotiate a contract constitutes involvement in the 
making of a contract under section 1090. (See City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 
Cal.App.3d 191, 196.) Further, where an existing contract requires periodic renegotiation of 
payment terms, the modification of those terms constitutes the making of a contract. (81 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 134, 136 (1998).) 
As a City officer, Councilmember Stewart is subject to Section 1090. Decisions relating to 
the potential extension of the expiring transit agreement between the City and Cal Poly, and those 
relating to a potential future modification of the existing emergency services agreement between the 
City and Cal Poly, involve contracts. The Councilmember would have a financial interest in each of 
those potential contracts because she is a salaried, exempt management employee of Cal Poly. 
Therefore, Section 1090 would prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in 
making, or the City from entering into, those potential contracts unless a remote interest or 
noninterest exception or the rule of necessity applies. 
The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where 
the financial interest involved is deemed to be a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a 
"noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. If a noninterest is present, the contract may be made 
without the officer's abstention, and a noninterest generally does not require disclosure. (City of 
Vernon v. Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515; 84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (2001).) 
Section 1091.5(a)(9) provides that an officer or employee has a noninterest in a public 
contract if his or her interest in that contract is: 
(9) That of a person receiving salary, per diem, or reimbursement for 
expenses from a government entity, unless the contract directly involves the 
department of the government entity that employs the officer or employee, 
provided that the interest is disclosed to the body or board at the time of 
consideration of the contract, and provided further that the interest is noted in 
its official record. 
In Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1081, the California Supreme Court 
determined that this exception applies when "the contract involves no direct financial gain, does not 
directly affect the official's employing department, and is only with the general government entity 
for which the official works." Thus, an official or employee has a noninterest in his or her 
government-entity employer under Section 1091.5(a)(9) provided that: (1) the contract at issue 
involves no direct financial gain to the official or employee, (2) the contract does not affect the 
official's or employee's department, (3) the interest is disclosed to the body or board at the time the 
contract is considered, and (4) the interest is noted in the body or board's official record. 


File No. A-19-039 
Page No. 5 
The Councilmember works as an Assistant Director for Personnel and Marketing in Cal 
Poly's Campus Health & Wellbeing Department. Her compensation from Cal Poly is not in any 
manner contingent upon or related to any relationship between the City and Cal Poly, and she is not 
anticipated to have any role in Cal Poly's negotiation or authorization of the potential extension of 
the expiring transit agreement or the potential modification of the existing emergency services 
agreement. Based on the facts presented, there is no indication t_hat either of those potential 
contracts would result in any direct financial gain to the Councilmember or would affect Cal Poly's 
Campus Health & Wellbeing Department. 
Therefore, Section 1090 would not prohibit the Councilmember from making or 
participating in making, or the City from entering into, either of those potential contracts because 
the noninterest exception set forth in Section 1091.5(a)(9) applies so long as the Councilmember 
discloses her interest in Cal Poly to the City Council at the time the potential contract is considered 
and notes the interest in the City Council's official record. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

City Council's official record.

that she is a salaried, exempt management employee of Cal Poly? 
(3) Does Section 1090 prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the making of, 
or the City from entering into:(A) The potential extension of an expiring transit agreement between the City and Cal Poly

services to Cal Poly students? 
(B) The potential future modification of an existing emergency services agreement between 
the City and Cal Poly governing the City's provision of fire and emergency medical services 
to Cal Poly?

CONCLUSION: (1) No. Cal Poly is not a "business entity" under the Act because it does not meet the definition of 
that term set forth in Section 82005. 
(2) No. The Councilmember does not have a source of income interest in Cal Poly under the Act

salary exception" to the definition of "income" set forth in Section 82030(b )(2).

City Council's official record.
